sentences.text: My name is Paula Mintz.
P-A-U-L-A Mintz, M-I-N-T-Z.
Is-- my maiden name is Herling, H-E-R-L-I-N-G.
Yes.
I was Pesa Herling.
P-E-S-A.
I was born the 3rd of October, 1919.
And where were you born?
I was born in Suchedniov near Kielce.
How do you spell that?
Suchedniov?
S-U-S-H-- C-H, excuse me, E-D-N-I-O-V.
This is Poland.
And this is [POLISH] or this is like region Kielce.
My father's name was Itzhak Mayer Herling.
I have very, very great memories of my father.
He was nicknamed the good Itzhak because he was doing so many good things to kids.
They loved him, Gentile and Jewish people.
And he had a soul of gold.
He did help lots of people.
And he was a very honest man, religious, but not fanatic.
He even kissed the ladies' hands.
Because we had all kinds of people in our business where my father belonged.
And he was very generous.
People knew him.
He was a very honest and humble man.
I have great memories of him.
Unfortunately, he died in the Treblinka.
So my heart is bleeding for that.
But I can't help it.
Yeah.
He was a clerk and expediter in a large iron factory.
And he also, later on, we had our own little business taking from-- we sold from a company, clay for porcelain.
And we send it with large-- from the large wagons from the-- with the train.
So we had this company.
And they liked my father very much.
And he gave them the clay.
He had clay mines.
He took the claims for free and gave it-- that's why we had it.
And my father made a living out of that.
He was born in Suchedniov, the same.
Suchedniov for a long time, maybe for hundreds of years.
We didn't know any other place.
This was our birthplace.
I was born and lived in the same place all my life and all my family.
We didn't move at all.
My mother's name was Hannah Rivka.
Stern.
Hannah Rivka Stern.
When she married my father, my father was a widower with two children, one son and a daughter.
But they didn't live with us.
They lived-- the daughter lived with my father's mother.
And the son was married, with three children.
And we were at home, eight children of us from the same mother and father.
In Radom.
No.
Never asked them that.
Yes I had, with me, six daughters-- six daughters and two brothers.
We were a very close family.
Each of us, when we grew up did something else.
I, for instance, was working in a lumber mill.
My older sisters were tutoring children from school.
And one was doing embroidery for all kinds of people.
And the other was younger than I am.
One was apprenticing in the-- by a good seamstress.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the other was still at home.
She was only a housewife, and a good one too.
I remember, she was getting up in the mornings, very early in the morning.
And she prepared us all for school.
We went off to school, looking after us very well.
I attended public school, seven classes.
And then I was apprenticeship-- in an apprenticeship in the lumber yard, for a year or so.
They taught me how to do it.
And then I worked full time, till the war broke out, as a bookkeeper.
No, it was only one school, one public school where I attended.
The language was Polish.
My favorite subject in school was grammatic and geography.
Oh, yes, very friendly, because we lived very close together.
They were my friends from, from childhood.
So we came to each other.
But some of them were nasty and did some special bad things to us.
They chased us around.
They, they spoke bad, bad language to us.
And they were very, very unhappy-- un, un-- they weren't nice.
They felt that this is-- we did bad things to them, the Jews.
It made feel me very bad.
I felt very bad.
Sometimes I came home from school crying.
But my mother, [POLISH] she says, don't take care of what they say.
You are what you are.
And you are, you should be proud of yourself.
So, we go and went on.
No, I don't know about that.
He wasn't because he was very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was-- everybody liked him.
He was a good man.
So he wasn't experiencing anything bad while he was working.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all that I know.
I never heard about any occurrences about this.
I can say that.
But the situation wasn't great.
Sometimes at home, we were-- my father was out, and somebody came in.
And they did some things wrong.
I can't recall.
But my mother told me that once she was chased after-- with a buggy and a horse.
And she fell down.
That's what I remember once happened, wintertime.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's all.
My hobbies when I was in the all the classes, like five, six, seven, I was very inclined with sports.
So I did--
All kinds of sports.
I was, I was loving to go out hikes, I liked to go for walks.
And I played volleyball.
I played other balls.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was a very alive girl.
Just we stayed home.
We couldn't afford a summer vacation to go out.
And besides, nobody did that at home.
Because our place was like a summer vacation.
People came to see-- to our city for vacations.
Because it was a very nice little city, with-- good for the sick people.
So they came from all over.
There were nice woods and [NON-ENGLISH].
So they came to our city.
And we have company with them.
We played with them together.
And it was pretty good summertime.
Did you have a large family in Suchedniov, your mother's family, your father's family?
My father-- mother's family, nobody was there.
Only my father's family.
In Radom.
My father-- my mother had two sisters in Radom and with families.
Quite often I went to Radom.
They came to us.
About 300, 400 miles.
I don't know exactly.
By train.
We went by train, yes.
And they came to us.
And I was quite a few times there by myself in Radom.
In Suchedniov.
Sometimes, sometimes, not very often, because we all had large families.
And there weren't any such big houses, like we have here.
We lived very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in two, two rooms, two or three, three rooms.
And we didn't have room for making big parties like that.
So everybody celebrated by themselves.
It was a great holiday.
My father went to shul.
And my mother prepared her nice food.
And we all were together, nobody missed at the table.
It wasn't supposed to be anybody missing.
We sat down together.
We sang songs after the dinner.
It was very traditional.
[LAUGHS] Traditional Jewish food.
Gefilte fish, or any other fish, soup with noodles, some good meat, and some dessert.
And then we had some fruit, fresh fruits.
And then we went out to play.
We came home.
We had a snack, and again, out to play.
And that's how it was.
In Suchedniov, the anti-Semitism was evident.
But where I lived, we lived very close, because we lived in a house where it belonged to Gentiles.
And they had big families.
And we have-- we were very close with each other.
So I did not feel that anti-Semitism so great.
But otherwise, I heard of other bad incidents with, with the Gentiles and Jews.
But I didn't take notice of that then, because we couldn't do differently.
We had to be in good terms with each other.
They came to our house, we came to them.
And they liked us.
No, they didn't mind our Gentile friends coming in the house.
They were very close with it-- even with my parents.
They liked us.
So my mother didn't have anything against it.
During the Christmas holidays it was better.
But when they had the heralds, the twelve days of the Israel days and they were clothed differently.
So they chased after us.
When-- in the evenings, that is.
So I didn't go out at all, these holidays.
Because they were coming to us and chasing us and scaring us very badly.
And we were young, we didn't understand.
So we were running away from them, and they were chasing us.
And so we were very afraid.
And during the Easter holidays, they weren't so, so sympathetic with the Jews.
Because they always claimed that the Jews took some blood for the matzah what we had.
And this was a very wrong idea what they had.
Because this was not true.
Your matzahs have to be straight made from water and flour, not anything else, not even salt, nothing.
So how could that be that we used to the blood from them?
This was a legend from years and years, centuries ago.
And they kept on believing this.
How would this anti-Jewish feeling be expressed?
They didn't speak to us too much.
They were a little further away from us.
And I don't remember any bad incidents about it but we could see the difference between it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's always-- but all in all, in our little town, it wasn't so bad.
I don't know exactly.
But I would think there were about 250 to 300 families in the whole little city.
Yes
There wasn't too much work for the Jewish people.
A lot of people had some little business.
A lot of people lived in very great poverty, the Jewish people.
And some people were working in lumber mills or there was another mill.
Some people did work-- were apprentices.
And there were a lot of cobblers and seamstresses.
This is, this was their profession.
A Jewish area?
Yeah?
Yeah, that was on the, in shtetl.
It called the shtetl.
And then it was, because the, the street was called [POLISH].
And there was, the Jews were located.
We had one, one shul.
There were few stiebel, where Jews came to daven.
But my father belonged to the shul.
And he was a member there.
And that's all what it was, there wasn't too much.
I wouldn't, no.
I don't remember.
But he had also a big family.
And the, his kids were friends even with my brother.
They were friendly people.
And they belonged-- they didn't belong sometimes to the shul.
They belonged to other organizations.
At home we spoke Yiddish.
But sometimes Polish, too, between us, between the sisters.
But mostly Yiddish.
And we knew the Polish language just the same as our-- my Polish girlfriends.
Exactly, or maybe better.
Because I was very good in grammar and so were my-- we were admired for that.
Yes, they had Zionist organizations, Mizrahi organizations.
I belonged to-- I went to [NON-ENGLISH], a Jewish school for girls only.
And that taught me a lot.
After public school, in the evenings.
Yeah, every evening we went there.
We performed some nice plays for each holiday, for, for Purim, for Pesach, for every-- every holiday we had a nice play.
Pardon me?
These plays were taken in the school where we, where we went, in the [NON-ENGLISH] mostly.
And the parents came to see.
It was very nice.
There were some.
We had one show, one movie show, that's all, where it took place in a big shop from the fire, fire department.
That's where they came to show us the shows.
A Jewish show, I don't remember.
But if one wants-- wanted to show-- to see a Jewish show, they had to go to a bigger city, like to Kielce, to Radom.
About 250 kilometers or maybe less.
I can't recall.
I wasn't interested in this then.
I was mostly interested in playing and in work.
And I didn't think of that.
Yes.
I read Jewish newspapers.
I read Jewish books.
I know one Jewish paper was 'The Moment.' One was [NON-ENGLISH].
And I even had a whole set of Sholem Aleichem's books, which I read.
Yeah.
Yes, there were people who left to Palestine then.
They belonged to kibbutzim.
And by us were a few kibbutzim, where people were working in the, in the mill where I was working.
They gave them jobs there.
And in other places from the kibbutzim, they came to work as women to clean the houses.
They worked very hard.
And they have their own commune.
And they waited to the-- after a period of time, they were going home.
And they were waiting for a certificate.
But sometimes they never got it.
And the war broke out, and they went-- they perished.
My one sister was married, during the war, 1939.
That was before the war, but in that year.
And she had one child.
Very nice.
It wasn't-- mostly it was outside the synagogue.
And then the, the service was in the synagogue.
But the canopy and everything was outside.
And then we went in for a, for a full meal at home or we-- on a bigger, big hall.
It was just like here sometimes.
But it wasn't so elaborate like here.
But people lived very nicely and very, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very happy.
Even so, it wasn't so elaborate and so rich like here.
I didn't hear at home about so many divorces like I hear here.
It was a good life.
I didn't have-- my childhood was very, very simple.
So was my other sisters.
We didn't live so as people live here, the children do here, no, very simple and plain.
But we, we were very close to each other.
And that meant a lot.
I wanted to work.
And so my father knew these people.
And he worked for them too.
What was the name of their company?
[NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] Lumber Mill.
And what did you do there?
First, as I said, they taught me how to be a bookkeeper, how to do managers.
And then I did the full job of bookkeeping-- prepared it for the accountant to come.
He came from Kielce.
And I was paying out the pay for the workers, paying out the pay for the, for the people who came with the big, big numbers.
And I was very efficient.
I never made mistakes.
They did really like me.
I couldn't even go out with another worker from, from that place and do, measure out a full load of, of lumber.
I was very good at it.
I saw as future that I'll stay there till the time comes, and I'll get married and go off.
Because I liked the job.
My father didn't let us go anywhere else.
He kept us together.
So I didn't see that I will go somewhere else.
And I enjoyed the job.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- [SIGHS] I liked it because it was not far from my house.
And my, my father came sometimes over to, to that place too, to have some, some kind of business, business doings.
So it was very good to see him there.
And they thought that I do a good job.
And they told him about me.
And he had [NON-ENGLISH].
